# Passage: 5  
Although the relationship between organization size and job rewards is complex, there seems to be agreement that while employees in large organizations have higher earnings, more fringe benefits, and more opportunities for promotion, they have less autonomy than do workers employed by small organizations. There is less agreement, however, about the reasons for these differences, and several factors may be involved.  
One of these factors is product markets. A popular theme among labor economists is that large organizations tend to be found in highly concentrated industries. Such organizations tend to have greater market power and hence higher “excess” profits. Profitability could help explain why large organizations tend to pay higher wages and provide better fringe benefits than small organizations.  
A second factor is labor markets. A labor shortage encourages firms to pay high wages and provide fringe benefits to attract job applicants. Compared to small organizations, large organizations may not only be more able to do this but also more motivated, mainly because they have more positions to fill. A shortage could also encourage employers to retain valued employees by providing opportunities for promotion, and, again, large organizations may be more able to do this. Another explanation involving labor markets rests on the assumption that small firms are more likely to be located in small communities. Thus, employees of small firms could have fewer opportunities for alternative employment than employees of large firms.  
A third factor is organizational structure. Large organizations are more complex and exhibit greater horizontal and vertical differentiation than small organizations. Greater differentiation, in turn, is associated with more opportunities for career advancement because there are more slots within the organization. The greater complexity and differentiation create a division of labor that is characterized by specialization. The interdependence that accompanies such specialization limits an individual’s autonomy because his or her actions must coordinate to a substantial degree with the actions of other employees. Also, large organizations often have more formalized relations with their employees, and formal rules and procedures tend to decrease employee autonomy.  
A fourth factor is job characteristics. Highly skilled and well-qualified workers tend to be paid more than less skilled and less qualified workers. They may also be granted more autonomy. Jobs in large organizations are more likely to require high-level skills not only because of the specialization mentioned earlier, but also because such organizations invest more in firm-specific training. Also, large organizations may want more qualified workers in order to reduce monitoring costs. Alternatively, insofar as monitoring costs are higher in large organizations and high wages accompany high-quality work, such organizations may pay higher wages than small organizations because larger ones are less able to accurately measure their employees’ performance. Such difficulty with monitoring may also account for greater employee autonomy.  
A fifth factor is unionization. Large organizations are more likely than small organizations to be unionized, and this may be why they pay high wages and provide better fringe benefits. Alternatively, large nonunionized organizations may provide their employees with high wages and fringe benefits in order to avoid unionization.
